coredata.dc:title: Indirect voltammetric determination of thiomersal in influenza vaccine using photo-degradation and graphene quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Pedrozo-Penafiel, Marlin J.
Miranda-Andrades, Jarol R.
Gutierrez-Beleño, Luis M.
Larrudé, Dunieskys G.
Aucelio, Ricardo Q.
coredata.dc:description: Thiomersal is an organomercury derivative that degrades producing thiosalicylic acid, dithiobenzoic acid and ethylmercury. It is widely used in topical pharmaceutical preparations and as preservative in vaccines and cosmetics. In this work, an electro-analytical method for thiomersal was developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a surface modifier of a glassy carbon electrode. The method rely on using square-wave voltammetry and exploring the synergistic effect between GQDs, visible radiation and the applied potential in producing very intense Hg oxidation peak during the anodic scan. A linear voltammetric response was obtained for the analyte in the concentration range from 3.0 μmol L−1 (1.2 μg mL−1) to 32 μmol L−1 (12 μg mL−1), with a detection limit of 0.9 μmol L−1 (0.34 μg mL−1). The proposed method was successfully applied for thiomersal determination in influenza vaccine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thiomersal
Mercury
Graphene quantum dots
Influenza vaccine
Voltammetry
Modified glassy carbon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of the glassy carbon electrode with GQDs
Analytical procedure for determination of Hg by multipass-cold vapor-AAS
Mercury cold vapor atomic absorption measurements
Characterization of GQDs in aqueous dispersion and modification of the GC
Cyclic voltammogram of thiomersal onto the GQDs-modified GC electrode
Electrochemical behavior of thiomersal on GC/GQDs electrode
Square-wave voltammetry using the GC/GQDs electrode: study of synergetic effect between applied potential and sample irradiation
Determination of thiomersal in influenza vaccines

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially financed by Coordenacao de Aperfeicoamento de Pessoal de Nivel Superior (CAPES-Brazil) ? Finance Code 001. Scholarships from Brazilian agencies FAPERJ (E-26/202.912/2017) and CNPq (303866/2017?9) and scientific grants from CNPq (409090/2018?2) and FAPERJ (E-26/010.101131/2018) are acknowledged. Authors are also thankful to Dra. Aurora P?rez-Gramatges (LASURF?DQ?PUC- Rio) for the contact angle experiments. Analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio) were used.

This work was partially financed by Coordenacao de Aperfeicoamento de Pessoal de Nivel Superior (CAPES-Brazil) – Finance Code 001. Scholarships from Brazilian agencies FAPERJ ( E-26/202.912/2017 ) and CNPq ( 303866/2017–9 ) and scientific grants from CNPq ( 409090/2018–2 ) and FAPERJ ( E-26/010.101131/2018 ) are acknowledged. Authors are also thankful to Dra. Aurora Pérez-Gramatges (LASURF–DQ–PUC- Rio) for the contact angle experiments. Analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio) were used.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Photoluminescence spectrum emission GQDs in aqueous dispersion (excitation at 357 nm). B) Raman spectra of GQDs. C) Scanning transmission electronic microscopy image of GQDs. D) Size distribution of GQDs in aqueous dispersion obtained by DLS with insert showing ξ-potential of GQDs in aqueous dispersion in function of pH.

Cyclic voltammograms of thiomersal (at 3.5 × 10−4 mol L−1) using a) GC/GQDs electrode and bare b) GC electrode: Peak 1 (E
p = +189 mV); peak 2 (E
p = +1004 mV; peak 3 (E
p = −33 mV. BR buffer pH 4.0 (0.02 mol L−1; KCl 0.25 mol L−1) and 100 mV s−1 scan rate.

A) Contact angle image of the surface of glassy carbon electrode modified with GQDs dispersed in water and in 1,4-dioxane. B) Histogram of the electrochemical response of thiomersal (n = 4) using GC electrode modified with GQDs obtained by hydro-exfoliation in water and then dispersed in either 1,4-dioxane and in water. C) Peak current (at +189 mV) in function of the amount of GQDs, produced by hydro-exfoliation of different masses of citric acid, deposited onto the GC. D) Cyclic voltammograms of thiomersal at different pH. a) pH 2, E
p = +184.1 mV, b) pH 3, E
p = +184.3 mV, c) pH 4, E
p = +189.0 mV, d) pH 5, E
p = +177.1 mV, e) pH 6, E
p = +175.6 mV, f) pH 7, E
p = +174.3 mV, g) pH 8, E
p = +169.8 mV, h) pH 9, E
p = +169.4 mV, i) pH 10, no signal. BR buffer 0.02 mol L−1/KCl 0.25 mol L−1 (scan rate at 100 mV s−1).

A) Cyclic voltammograms of thiomersal (at 2 × 10−4 mol L −1) using the GC/GQDs electrode at different scan rates (10–900 mV s−1). B) peak current (I
p ) in function of square root of the scan rate (v
1/2 ) with R2 = 0.990. C) Cyclic voltammogram of thiomersal performed within the limited potential range between −200 and + 600 mV. D) I
p1
/I
p3 ratio in function of the scanning rate for the redox pair (peak 1 and 3) obtained in cyclic voltammetry. Experiments made with BR buffer pH 4.0 (0.02 mol L−1/KCl 0.25 mol L−1).

SWV response from thiomersal (2 × 10−4 mol L −1) using GC/GQDs electrode. A) Measurements made (a) in the dark and (b) under exposition to visible radiation. B) Measurements made (a) in the dark, (b) in the dark after 20 cyclic voltammetric cycles and (c) under exposition to visible radiation after 20 cyclic voltammetric cycles. C) Comparative of analytical signals obtained by SWV using different experimental conditions. BR Buffer 0.02 mol L−1 (pH 4.0) with KCl 0.25 mol L−1; SWV scanning rate at 100 mV s−1 and voltammetric cycles at 100 mV s−1 from ±300 mV.

A) SWV of thiomersal using GC/GQDs electrode: a) 0, b) 4 × 10−6, c) 8 × 10−6, d) 1.2 × 10−5, e) 1.6 × 10−5, f) 2 × 10−5, g) 2.4 × 10−5, h) 2.8 × 10−5, i) 3.2 × 10−5 mol L−1, and B) analytical curve: I
p = (6.47 ± 0.23) × 105 C
thiomersal + (11.1 ± .4.4) (R2 = 0.991).
Analysis results of thiomersal in vaccines using the proposed SWV method and by using cold vapor-AAS. Standard deviation for n = 3. Confidence limit of 95%; two tails, each with α/2 = 0.025.

head.ce:title: Indirect voltammetric determination of thiomersal in influenza vaccine using photo-degradation and graphene quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro-RJ, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pontifícia Universidade Católica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Pontificia Universidade Catolica do Rio de Janeiro (PUC-Rio), Rio de Janeiro-RJ, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MackGraphe, Graphene and Nanomaterial Research Center, Mackenzie Presbyterian University, São Paulo, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MackGraphe, Graphene and Nanomaterial Research Center, Mackenzie Presbyterian University, Sao Paulo, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thiomersal is an organomercury derivative that degrades producing thiosalicylic acid, dithiobenzoic acid and ethylmercury. It is widely used in topical pharmaceutical preparations and as preservative in vaccines and cosmetics. In this work, an electro-analytical method for thiomersal was developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a surface modifier of a glassy carbon electrode. The method rely on using square-wave voltammetry and exploring the synergistic effect between GQDs, visible radiation and the applied potential in producing very intense Hg oxidation peak during the anodic scan. A linear voltammetric response was obtained for the analyte in the concentration range from 3.0 μmol L−1 (1.2 μg mL−1) to 32 μmol L−1 (12 μg mL−1), with a detection limit of 0.9 μmol L−1 (0.34 μg mL−1). The proposed method was successfully applied for thiomersal determination in influenza vaccine.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiomersal is an organomercury derivative widely used in topical pharmaceutical preparations and as preservative in vaccines and cosmetics [1]. Thiomersal structure comprises of 49.55% (w/w) of mercury and its degradation produces thiosalicylic acid, dithiobenzoic acid and ethylmercury [2]. Thiomersal has been used in vaccines against hepatitis B, influenza, Haemophilusinfluenzae type b, diphtheria, tetanus and whooping cough [3] in typical quantities between 45 and 55 μg in a 0.50 mL per dose [4]. Despite the fact that thiomersal is a very effective preservative, its inclusion in vaccines is controversial as some studies indicated the presence of mercury residues in human tissues that could affect the central nervous, cardiovascular, renal and immune systems [4,5]. Therefore, thiomersal concentration in vaccines must be kept at safety levels in order to avoid unnecessary exposition of humans. In this context, analytical methods are required to enable an easy determination of thiomersal at trace levels in order to allow, for instance, the quality control of vaccines.

Gil et al. photo-decomposed thiomersal in solution using UV and H2O2. Further reduction of Hg2+ to Hg was achieved, in a second step, by ultrasound after addition of formic acid. Mercury cold vapor was carried to the quartz cell of an atomic absorption spectrometer to be detected at levels down to 40 μg L−1 (0.09 μmol L−1) [6]. Reverse-phase high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled with atomic fluorescence spectrometry was used for the determination of thiomersal. Mercury cold vapor was generated by the post-column reduction of mercury species using UV and in the presence of formic acid. Thimerosal was converted to Hg2+ then reduced to Hg. The method was applied to the determination of thimerosal in samples of a pharmaceutical industry effluent and in river waters. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained was 0.09 g L−1 (200 μmol L−1) [7]. Xu et al. proposed an atomic fluorescence spectrometric method for the determination of thimerosal in the vaccines based on Fe3+-induced degradation of thiomersal and LOD of 0.03 μg L−1 (0.07 nmol L−1) was obtained [8]. Wu et al. produced mercury cold vapor that was carried to an atomic fluorescence spectrometer. The method was applied for vaccine samples with LOD of 0.06 μg L−1 (0.1 nmol L−1) [9]. Dos Santos et al. described the determination of Hg, originally present as thimerosal, in vaccines by photochemical mercury cold vapor generation coupled to inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP OES) enabling LOD of 0.6 μg L−1 (1 nmol L−1) [10]. He et al. also described an ICP OES method to determine thimerosal in vaccines by photochemical cold vapor generation (LOD of 0.17 μg L−1 or 0.4 nmol L−1) [11]. Campanella et al. used flow injection analysis (FIA) for the determination of thiomersal based on the UV/microwave induced online oxidation of organic mercury, followed by atomic fluorescence detection [12]. The method produced LOD of 0.003 μg mL−1 (7 nmol L−1) and it was applied to ophthalmic solutions. Miranda-Andrades et al. reported a method for quantification of thiomersal, after photo-degradation assisted by graphene quantum dots (GQDs), converting thiomersal into ethylmercury then to Hg2+, using visible radiation [13]. Detection was made after chemical reduction to produce mercury cold vapor using a dedicated mercury portable atomic absorption spectrometer. The method enabled LOD of 20 ng L−1 (0.049 nmol L−1) in aqueous effluents of pharmaceutical industry and in urine samples.

Meyer et al. used HPLC with absorption photometric detection to determine thiomersal in ophthalmic solutions and vaccines detecting values as low as 0.025 μg (0.06 nmol) in 50 μL of sample [14]. Thiomersal was derivatized with dithizone before carrying out the analysis by HPLC [15]. Acosta et al. used HPLC with fluorimetric detection (315/442 nm) to determine thimerosal in pharmaceutical industry effluents after it was converted (in an on-line UV reactor) to thiosalycilic acid enabling LOD of 0.72 μg mL−1 (1.8 μmol L−1) [16]. Shivastaw et al. developed a colorimetric method (measurement at 538 nm) based on the reaction between thiomersal and dithizone in basic medium to analyze vaccines, obtaining LOD of 200 μg L−1 (0.49 μmol L−1) [17]. Fredj et al. used a FIA system to produce mercury cold vapor from thiomersal, which was separated in a gas diffusion cell then passing through a permanganate solution where Hg was oxidized to Hg2+ with the decreasing the permanganate absorbance at 528 nm. The method was applied for vaccine samples with LOD of 70 μg L−1 (0.17 μmol L−1) [18].

A limited number of works has been dedicated to the electroanalytical determination of thiomersal. Birner et al. proposed the use of polarography to determine thiomersal in aqueous solution and in vaccines where the analyte was present in amounts above 20 μg g−1 [19]. Da Silva et al. applied cyclic voltammetry with glassy carbon (GC) electrode (acetate buffer; pH 4.0) to determine thiomersal measuring the oxidation peak at +760 mV. Analyses of contact lens care solutions were made with LOD of 0.09 μg mL−1 (0.22 μmol L−1) [2]. Differential pulse voltammetry was used by Piech et al. for thiomersal determination in vaccines using a mercury film silver-based electrode (phosphate buffer; pH 7.0) enabling LOD of 0.36 μg L−1 (0.9 nmol L−1) based on the peak at −555 mV [19]. Gonzalez et al. reported a method for determination of thiomersal by square-wave voltammetry (SWV) using a screen-printed electrode modified with chitosan (phosphate buffer; pH 3.2), enabling LOD of 15.3 μg L−1 (0.038 μmol L−1). The sensor was used to analyze human urine and commercial vaccines. The modified electrode promoted the oxidation of thiomersal with peak at +240 mV [4]. Penagos-Llanos et al. applied SWV for determination of thiomersal in vaccines and in pharmaceuticals using a carbon-paste modified electrode decorated with La2O3 (using phosphate buffer at pH 3.1), enabling detection limit of 36 μg L−1 (0.09 μmol L−1). The anodic scan produced a thiomersal peak in the range between +800 and + 900 mV [20]. Wei et al. developed a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on a microporous metal–organic-framework for the detection of thiomersal. A monolayer of 4-aminothiophenol (4-ATP) was self-assembled on the surface of a gold electrode, followed by electro-polymerization of the 4-ATP-functionalized gold nanoparticles in the presence of template (thiomersal), forming a microporous molecularly imprinted polymeric film [21]. Thimerosal was determined in ophthalmic solutions and LOD was 0.014 ng L−1 (0.035 pmol L−1).

In this work, an electro-analytical method for thiomersal was developed using GQDs as a surface modifier of a GC electrode. GQDs are nanometric fragments of graphene where electron transport is confined in their dimensions. GQDs present special optical and catalytic properties, due to quantum confinement, that can be accessed in the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum. The research presents a new approach for the determination of thiomersal in influenza vaccines using SWV and GQDs-modified GC (GC/GQDs) electrode, exploring the synergistic effect between GQDs, visible radiation and the applied potential in producing very intense Hg oxidation peak during anodic scan.

Voltammetric experiments were made using a potentiostat μ-AUTOLAB Type III (Metrohm, The Netherlands) operating in square-wave voltammetry and cyclic voltammetry modes. A three-electrode system was used with a working glassy carbon (GC) electrode modified with GQDs (GC/GQDs electrode), a saturated Ag|AgCl(KCl(sat)) reference electrode and a Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode. The working cell, made of Pyrex glass (15 mL), was adapted to a Teflon cap with four hole-positions to place the electrodes and the purging gas. Total carbon measurements were made on a carbon analyzer model TOC-VCPN (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence measurements were made on a model LS 55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer) (600 nm min−1 scan rate, 10.0 nm spectral bandpass and 1 cm optical path length quartz cuvettes). Mercury measurements were made by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) using a mercury cold vapor dedicated system with a multi-path length cell (multipass-cold vapor -AAS), model RA-915 (LUMEX, Russia), equipped with Zeeman background correction and connected to an RP-92 accessory for chemical reduction in aqueous solution.

Images from the GQDs were made using a field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, model JSM-7800F, Japan) operated in the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode at 30 kV. Raman spectroscopy was performed using a confocal Raman microscope (WITec Alpha 300R, Germany) equipped with an electron multiplying charge coupled device detector and an air cooled solid-state laser. Raman measurements were obtained using a 532 nm laser source, a monochromator with a 600 line/mm grating and 1 mW of laser radiation power. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential measurements were made on a nanoparticle analizer model SZ-100 (Horiba, Japan). Zeta potential measurement were made using an acrylic electrochemical cell containing a flat carbon electrode (6 mm thickness) and DLS measurements were obtained using glass cuvettes with 1 cm optical path length. The contact angle measurements were made using drop shape analyzer- Kruss Advance.

Measurements of pH were made with a pHmeter model mPA 210 from Tecnopon (Brazil) with a glass membrane electrode conjugated with a Ag|AgCl(KCl(sat) reference electrode. The ultrasonic bath (9 L) was a NSC 2800 model from Unique, Brazil. The laboratory-made photochemical reactor consisted of six commercial fluorescent lamps (6 W each, emitting in the visible spectral range) set on the internal wall of a PVC tube (20 cm diameter × 30 cm width) fixed onto a wood box, as a base that contain a magnetic stirrer which promotes mass transport within the electrochemical cell. A scheme of the experimental set-up used for the determination of thiomersal is presented in Fig. S1.

All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (resistivity < 18 MΩ cm) obtained from a water purifier Milli-Q Gradient System A10, Millipore (USA). Thiomersal, thiosalicylic acid and 2,2′dithiodibenzoic acid were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Ethylmercury was from Brooks Rand Instruments (USA). Sodium hydroxide (98.0% m/m), acetic acid (65% m/v), boric acid (99.8% m/m), phosphoric acid (85%, m/v), potassium chloride, acetate sodium, and citric acid were obtained from Merck (Germany). The 1,4-dioxane was from Tedia (USA). Tin chloride (SnCl2) and dimethyl sulfoxide were from Vetec (Brazil). Aluminum oxide suspension (1.0 μm) was obtained from Fortel, Brazil. Nitrogen gas (99.999% purity) was from Messer gases (Brazil). PTFE syringe filters (0.45 μm) were from Whatman (UK). Dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight of 3.5 kDa) was from Spectrum Laboratories Inc. (USA).

Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer stock solutions were prepared at 0.04 mol L−1 by mixing acetic acid, boric acid and phosphoric acid, then adjusting to pH 4 by the addition of small aliquots of NaOH solution (1 mol L−1). A stock electrolyte solution KCl (0.5 mol L−1) was also prepared in water.

The GQDs were produced by the pyrolysis and hydro-exfoliation of citric acid (2 g). First, 2 g of citric acid was placed into a 5 mL beaker and heated to about 240 °C using a heating plate. As citric acid melted (with color changing from colorless to pale yellow and then to brown) within 2–5 min, the hot molten material was added into 25 mL of ultrapure water, at room-temperature, in order to obtain (after filtering on a 0.22 μm syringe filter) a clear pale yellow aqueous dispersion that was further dialyzed, for 24 h, in order to obtain the so called GQDs aqueous synthesis dispersion. GQDs dispersions were also prepared by adding the molten material into HCl (0.1 mol L −1) and NaOH (0.25 mol L −1) aqueous solutions at room-temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of the glassy carbon electrode with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs aqueous synthesis dispersion (25 mL) was evaporated to about 200 μL, then 2 mL of 1,4-dioxane was added and the mixture was briefly vortex agitated. After ultrasonic agitation, for 20 min, the dispersion was stored at room temperature. The GC electrode surface was polished with alumina then, washed with ultra-pure water prior to modification. Modification consisted on adding 10 μL of the GQDs dispersed in dioxane (previously homogenized in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min) onto the GC surface. The 1,4 dioxane rapidly evaporated, at room temperature, allowing the formation of a homogeneous layer of GQDs on the electrode surface.

Cyclic voltammetry was used to study the redox process with a scanning velocity varying from 10 to 900 mV s−1 in the ±1500 mV potential range. The supporting electrolyte consisted of a mixture of the BR buffer solution (final concentration in the cell of 0.02 mol L−1 within the pH range from 2 to 10) and KCl solution (final concentration in the cell of 0.25 mol L−1). Square-wave voltammetry (SWV) was employed to study the redox process and to perform quantitative determination of thiomersal using BR buffer solution (pH 4.0; 0.02 mol L−1 and KCl 0.25 mol L−1). Quantification was performed by measuring peak height, at +158 mV, using anodic scanning within ±1500 mV at 25 Hz using 20 mV step potential and 40 mV pulse amplitude.

The influenza vaccines (FluQuadri®), used in the Brazilian public health system, was fortified with thiomersal at 2.47 × 10−4 mol L−1 (97.1 μg mL−1). The influenza vaccine with thiomersal, GC Flu Multi (Split virion, inactivated) Green Cross, was obtained from the Colombian public health system, and contained originally 0.01% m/v (100 μg mL−1) of thiomersal according to the vaccine instructions [22]. Each vaccine sample was diluted in ultrapure water prior to the addition of the electro-analytical analytical cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure for determination of Hg by multipass-cold vapor-AAS
Mercury cold vapor atomic absorption measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The cold vapor multipass-AAS system was used to measure the mercury formed during the electrochemical process. In order to do this, samples collected from the electrochemical cell (5 mL) were diluted 1000 times and aliquots (10 mL) were transferred to the bubbler of the cold vapor-AAS system (containing 2 mL SnCl2 (20% v/v) to perform chemical reduction and the absorbance measurement of the mercury cold vapor produced form the reduction of Hg2+.

Analyzes by multipass-cold vapor-AAS were made based on literature [13]. Sample solutions (100 μL) were transferred to glass test tubes containing 10.0 mL of a H2O2 (1%) solution (with pH previously adjusted to 4.5), also containing 17 μL of the dispersion of GQDs. The test tube content was transferred to a glass reaction cell containing 2 mL of a SnCl2 aqueous solution (20% v/v) in order to provide a rapid reduction to Hg, that was transferred, by an air flow passing though the solution, to the multipass-cold vapor-AAS system operating in a continuous acquisition mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs in aqueous dispersion and modification of the GC
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs in aqueous dispersion (pH 6.4) were characterized using different techniques. The GQDs dispersion showed photoluminescence emission with maximum at 445 nm (Fig. 1 A), when excited at 357 nm, indicating quantum confinement. There is no change in emission maximum as the excitation was varied by ±20 nm. Raman spectra of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1B, exhibiting typical graphene D and G-bands at 1349 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1 respectively. Characteristic Raman features (bands D and G) indicated sp2 carbon in graphitic nanostructures. The total carbon content in the original aqueous GQDs dispersion, prepared by hydro-exfoliation of 2 g of citric acid, was about 580 mg L−1.

As the GQDs original aqueous dispersion is somewhat viscous, a dilution with water (3 parts water to 1 part of GQDs original dispersion, in volume) was required to obtain the STEM images (Fig. 1C). It was observed nanomaterials with an average diameter of 35.9 ± 2.2 nm. DLS confirmed this diameter value with a hydrodynamic size of 34.0 ± 3.4 nm (Fig. 1D). The closer estimated sizes (by the image analysis and by DLS) is explained as GQDs are not surface modified with any ligands as usually are the inorganic semiconductor QDs. The negative charge of the GQDs in aqueous dispersion increases as the pH value increases, as seen by ζ-potential values in insert in Fig. 1D, indicating lower aggregation of nanomaterials as the pH of the aqueous dispersion increases. This is due probably to the –COOH groups, at the border of the GQDs, losing protons and becoming negatively charged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammogram of thiomersal onto the GQDs-modified GC electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
A preliminary study, using GQDs dispersion prepared with 2 g of citric acid, was made to evaluate the effect of GQDs modification onto glassy carbon (GC) on the electrochemical response of thiomersal (at 3.5 × 10−4 mol L−1). Cyclic voltammograms were obtained comparing the use of bare GC and the GC modified with 10 μL of the GQDs dispersion (re-suspended in 1,4-dioxane). Using a supporting electrolyte at pH 4.0 (BR buffer solution at 0.02 mol L−1/KCl at 0.25 mol L−1) it was observed two anodic peaks, one with maximum potential (E
p ) at +189 mV and a large one with E
p at +1004 mV, as well as one cathodic peak, in the reversed scan, with E
p at +33 mV (Fig. 2 ). The position of these peaks remains the same no matter the GC was modified with GQDs or not. However, the intensity of these peaks increased when surface of GC is modified with GQDs. For instance, the main peak, observed at E
p = +189 mV (related to the oxidation of Hg0 to Hg2+) was about 3.3 times more intense using the GC/GQDs electrode. The modification with GQDs might have improved contact between electrode and analyte in solution, leading to a larger measured current from the redox processes concerning thiomersal. As potential shifts were not observed, there is no evidence of electrocatalytic effect induced by the namomaterial when on the surface of the electrode. It is important to point out that blank solution presents none of these electrochemical peaks (Fig. S2).

Da Silva et al. [2] reported the oxidation mechanism of thiomersal using acetate buffer (pH 4.0) in aqueous solutions with initial formation of thiosalicylic acid and ethylmercury [23,24]. Thiosalicylic acid is subsequently oxidized to dithiobenzoic acid while ethylmercury (II) hydroxide is reduced to elemental mercury (scheme in Fig. S3) [23,24]. Ethylmercury may form complex ions such as CH3CH2Hg)2OH+ or CH3CH2HgOH depending upon pH [25] affecting Hg/Hg2+ the redox process. Experiments performed with these chemical species using cyclic voltammetry and the GC/GQDs electrode are shown in Fig. S4.

The cyclic voltammogram of an Hg2+ (2 × 10−4 mol L−1) standard solution produced a redox pair (E
anodic
/E
catodic = +168/+14 mV) that corresponds to the redox pair observed for thiomersal at E
anodic
/E
catodic = +189/+33 mV. The large peak of thiomersal at +1004 mV is probably a mixture of anodic events concerning thiosalicylic acid (E
p at +464 mV and also at +1233 mV) and the one of dithiobenzoic acid (E
p = +1287 mV).

The stability of a modified electrode is dependent on achieving homogeneous distribution of the modifying material on the surface of the electrode [26]. Therefore, finding the appropriate solvent to produce the ink used to be added on the surface of the GC allows greater durability as well as stability and reproducibility of the electrode response. Modification by adding 10 μL of the dispersion (re-suspended in 1,4-dioxane) was found adequate since good coverage of GC surface was obtained. Solvent might favor high adhesion strength between the ink and the electrode, affecting the descent of modifying material on the electrode surface [27]. Aside from electrode/solvent interactions, electrode/modifying material and solvent/modifying material interactions govern the aggregation size, modifying material arrangement that influences electron transport in an electrode system [26].

A contact angle measurement (Fig. 3 A) has been made to evaluate the interaction between the ink of GQDs, dispersed in either 1,4-dioxane or water, and the GC electrode surface. The contact angle between the surface of the GC and the GQD dispersed in 1,4-dioxane is significantly smaller, when compared to the aqueous GQDs dispersion. A smaller contact angle provides better surface coverage and stability of the GQDs-solvent on the electrode surface. The hydrophobic interactions of 1,4-dioxane with the electrode surface are greater than the interactions of water with the surface electrode, that is, the GQDs-1,4-dioxane has a better adhesion to the electrode surface. In addition, 1,4-dioxane has high vapor pressure in relation to water, making it an excellent solvent to be used in the preparation of the ink as rapid evaporation (Fig. 3A) promotes better adhesion of GQDs on the electrode surface. Sequential electrochemical measurements (n = 20) were made at E
p = +158 mV using anodic SWV (respective to the anodic peak at E
p = +189 mV obtained by cyclic voltammetry) of a thiomersal solution (at 2.4 × 10−5 mol L−1) using modified GQDs/GC electrodes (Fig. 3B). It was found that the response obtained from the electrode modified using ink of GQDs dispersed in 1.4-dioxane was 1.5 times more intense than the one observed using ink of GQDs dispersed in water. Relative standard deviation of the measured signals was also significantly better using ink of GQDs dispersed in 1.4-dioxane (4.9%) when compared with the one observed using ink of GQDs dispersed in water (28.3%).

A comparative evaluation was made by GC modification with the original GQDs dispersions prepared by hydro-exfoliation using different masses of citric acid (1, 2 and 3 g). After concentration (though evaporation) and re-dispersion in 1,4-dioxane, it was found that the electrode modified with the dispersion, originally prepared using 2 g of citric acid, produced a signal (measured at E
p = +189 mV using cyclic voltammetry) about twice the intensity of the one observed using the electrode modified with dispersion prepared using 1 g of citric acid (Fig. 3C). The GC modification using the dispersion obtained from hydro-exfoliation of 3 g of citric acid produced greater variation of results besides the fact that the ink did not properly dried on the surface of the electrode after evaporation of 1,4-dioxane. The GC modification with dispersion obtained originally from hydro-exfoliation of 2 g of citric acid produced a surface modification with mechanical stability and reproducible response over time.

The choice of the pH to perform voltammetry was made based on the intensity of the Hg/Hg2+ oxidation peak measured from thiomersal (4.7 × 10−5 mol L−1) varying the pH of the BR buffer of the supporting electrolyte from 2.0 to 10.0. Voltammograms in Fig. 3D show that the anodic peak concerning mercury oxidation to Hg2+ is affected due to the favorable formation of Hg(OH)2 or mercuric hydroxi-complexes at pH above 7.0. As pH decreased from 6.0 to 4.0, the measured current increased and E
p slightly shifted from +175.6 mV to +189.0 mV. Then, at more acidic conditions, peak intensity decreases, therefore pH 4.0 was chosen for quantitative studies concerning thiomersal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of thiomersal on GC/GQDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
By means of the GC/GQDs electrode, the intensity of the main anodic peak, obtained from the cyclic voltammetry of thiomersal (at E
p = +189 mV). increases proportionally with the increase of the square root of the scan rate (v
1/2 ), as seen in Fig. 4 A. The equation modeling this response, obtained using least squares linear regression (Fig. 4B), was I
p = (35.2 ± 1.16)v
1/2 - (1.04 ± 0.21) × 10−4 (R2 = 0.990). This result indicated that the process is controlled by diffusion.

The dependency between the sharp and most intense anodic peak, E
p = +189 mV, and the one obtained in the reversed scan, at E
p = +33 mV, was proven by performing a cyclic voltammetry within the potential range between −200 and + 600 mV (Fig. 4C). The quasi-reversible nature of this Hg/Hg2+ redox pair was confirmed based on the criteria proposed by Nicholson and Shain [28] considering the ratio of current intensities (I
p1
/I
p3 ), where I
p1 is the anodic peak current and I
p3 is the cathodic peak current. As I
p1
/I
p3 decreases exponentially as the scanning rate (v) is increased, the quasi-reversibility of the electrochemical process is proven (Fig. 4D). This is in agreement with Rhadi et al. [29] who reported quasi-reversibility of the Hg/Hg2+ redox process at relatively high scan velocities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Square-wave voltammetry using the GC/GQDs electrode: study of synergetic effect between applied potential and sample irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
For quantitative purposes, thiomersal was determined by measuring the sharp Hg/Hg2+ redox peak that appears at E
p = +158 mV. As literature reports that GQDs, under exposition to visible spectral radiation, favors rapid photo-degradation of thiomersal into Hg2+ [13], SWV experiments were also made inside a photo-reactor in order to evaluate a synergetic effect between applied potential and radiation that could lead to a larger analytical signals. In Fig. 5 A the SWV response of thiomersal measured using the GC/GQDs electrode in an electrochemical cell inside a photo-reactor is shown. The signal, measured was only 18% higher when voltammetric scanning was made after 20 min of exposition to visible light when compared to the one measured with the electrochemical cell in the dark (Fig. 5A). This indicates that visible light was not very effective in promoting the direct degradation of thiomersal when GQDs is immobilized onto the surface of GC. A second experiment was performed by applying a sequence of 20 voltammetric cycles (at 100 mV s−1 within ±300 mV) under exposition of light, before applying the square-wave voltammetric scan to read the Hg/Hg2+ peak. Cyclic voltammograms were applied without solution stirring in order to minimize mass transport of analyte away from the electrode/solution interface, allowing accumulation of produced CH3CH2Hg from thiomersal [2]. Contrary to what was observed with thiomersal, it was found that the influence of visible radiation improved the GQDs mediated degradation of CH3CH2Hg into (CH3CH2)2Hg and Hg then to Hg2+, leading to an improvement of 2.8 times of the signal measured when performing the direct SWV scan without exposition to light (Fig. 5B). A comparative of analytical signals obtained by SWV using different experimental conditions is shown in Fig. 5C. It was found that the SWV signal, acquired using the GC/GQDs after 20 voltammetric cycles and under light exposition, was respectively 4.5 and 2.8 times more intense than the ones observed after the direct SWV scan in the dark using GC and GC/GQDs electrode. The influence of the previous voltammetric cycles and the incidence of light became evident as the signal achieved under such circumstances was 2.3 times more intense than the one observed under exposition of radiation but without the voltammetric cycles and 1.4 times more intense than the one using previous voltammetric cycles but performed in the dark. Experiments using bare GC exposed to radiation and applied potential were also performed. The results showed that there was no significant increasing in the signal measured at E
p = +158 mV, without the presence of GQDs.

The number of voltammetric cycles required prior to produce the SWV signal, under light exposure, was studied and results are shown in Fig. S5, indicating 20 voltammetric cycles as the number to produce better results. A larger number of cycles was not attempted since it would increase analysis time also allowing significant diffusion of produced mercury species away from the electrode/solution interface.

A further experiment was made using cold vapor-AAS with multi-path length detection cell in order to evaluate the production of Hg and Hg2+ under different experimental conditions in the electrochemical cell (Fig. S6). Aliquots of solutions (supporting electrolyte with and without the addition of thiomersal) and solutions of thiomersal, in supporting electrolyte, submitted to different treatments in the electrochemical cell were collected, diluted and introduced into de reaction cell (containing SnCl2) connected to the cold vapor-AAS system. Background mercury level (7 ng L−1) was found when the diluted supporting electrolyte was added to the cell, increasing to 12 ng L−1 as thiomersal was added to the supporting electrolyte solution before dilution and analysis by cold vapor-AAS. This indicates that a trace level residue of Hg2+ is found in thiomersal solution as consequence of natural degradation. It was also found that a larger amount (more than 3 times) of mercury was measured after applying 20 voltammetric cycles (under stirring of solution) and one SWV scan using GC/GQDs than using bare GC. It is important to point out that most of the elemental mercury generated during the electrochemical process is lost during the time to perform sample cycling under stirring, sample collection from cell, dilution, and transferring to the cold vapor-AAS system. Therefore, the mercury measured by cold vapor-AAS in the experiments made with solutions from the electrochemical cell is the fraction of mercury formed as Hg2+. The importance of exposition to light in the electrode modified with GQDs is also demonstrated as the measured mercury was about 2 times the one observed without incidence of light.

Experimental conditions to perform electrochemical measurement of thiomersal using the GC/GQDs electrode were studied. Pulse amplitude was varied (from 30 to 90 mV) with 40 mV selected due to the better relationship between peak sharpness and intensity. Pulse frequency (f) of 25 Hz was also a compromise between peak current and peak width within the 5–200 Hz range. Potential step was studied within the 2–20 mV with 2 mV preferred considering the higher intensity achieved.

The analytical curve was constructed by sequential addition of increasing concentration of analyte into the electrochemical cell with SWV voltammograms shown in Fig. 6 A and analytical curve displayed in Fig. 6B. Analytical curve equation in terms of peak current (I
p ) was I
p = (6.47 ± 0.23) × 105 C
thiomersal + (11.1 ± 4.4), where I
p is in μA and C
thiomersal is the analyte concentration in mol L−1. Determination coefficient (R2) was 0.991 and linear dynamic range expanded up to 32 μmol L−1 (12.9 μg mL−1). Instrumental LOD and LOQ were calculated respectively as 3sb m−1 and 10sb m−1, where s
b is the standard deviation (n = 10) of the I
p measured for the lowest concentration of the analytical curve and m is the sensitivity of the curve. For the GC/GQDs electrode, the instrumental LOD was 0.9 μmol L−1 (0.34 μg mL−1) and the instrumental LOQ was 2.8 μmol L−1 (1.1 μg mL−1) of thiomersal. Instrumental precision was obtained as the coefficient of variation from 10 consecutive measurements of the signal produced by the analyte standard at 1 × 10−5 mol L−1 (4.0 μg mL−1) with each one of the measurements made after solution agitation to replenish the solution-electrode interface. Instrumental precision obtained was 3.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thiomersal in influenza vaccines
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitation of thiomersal in vaccines was performed using the proposed method (Table 1 ). One of the influenza vaccines, the one without thiomersal, was fortified with the analyte at 247 μmol L−1 (97.1 μg mL−1). The other one was directly analyzed to access the level of thiomersal originally present. Three aliquots of these samples were analyzed. The recoveries obtained were 91.9 ± 2.0% for the fortified vaccine and 92.4 ± 2.7% for the vaccine originally containing thiomersal (equivalent to 100 μg mL−1 as reported by manufacturer). As the composition of vaccines, besides the virus antigens and water, consists of sodium and potassium chlorides and sodium and potassium dihyrogen phosphates [22], interference studies were not conducted as the salt content added from sample is less than the one of the supporting electrolyte in the cell.

These same samples were also analyzed (three replicates) by cold vapor-AAS using the method reported by Miranda-Andrades [13] and recoveries were 87.8 ± 1.3% for the fortified vaccine and 99.2 ± 1.3% for the vaccine with thiomersal in its composition (Table 1). Despite differences in the mean values, the Student t-test for similar variances (confidence limit of 95%; two tails, each with α/2 = 0.025) indicated that results are in statistical agreement. The obtained results lead to the conclusion that the proposed method can be use for a simple, selective and sensitive determination of thiomersal in vaccines samples.

The proposed method allowed sensitive indirect determination of thiomersal using GQDs as a modifier of a GC electrode. By combining exposition of light with the applied potential promoted a significant degradation of thiomersal and the increasing of the Hg/Hg2+ redox peak used as analytical signal measured by SWV. The GQDs is an attractive nanomaterial for sensing devices due to their easy preparation, photo-catalytic properties, high thermal stability, and low cost. Modification of GC is simple and the adaptation of the electro-analytical cell into a photochemical reactor is very simple. The method was successfully applied for the determination of thiomersal concentration in vaccines and proven as sensitive as the commonly used methods. A linear voltammetric response was obtained in the concentration range from 3.0 μmol L−1 (1.2 μg mL−1) to 32 μmol L−1 (12 μg mL−1), with a detection limit of 0.9 μmol L−1 (0.34 μg mL−1). Acceptable recoveries confirms the usefulness of the method for quality control.

Marlin J. Pedrozo-Penafiel: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Jarol R. Miranda-Andrades: Formal analysis. Luis M. Gutierrez-Beleño: Formal analysis. Dunieskys G. Larrudé: Formal analysis. Ricardo Q. Aucelio: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially financed by Coordenacao de Aperfeicoamento de Pessoal de Nivel Superior (CAPES-Brazil) – Finance Code 001. Scholarships from Brazilian agencies FAPERJ (E-26/202.912/2017) and CNPq (303866/2017–9) and scientific grants from CNPq (409090/2018–2) and FAPERJ (E-26/010.101131/2018) are acknowledged. Authors are also thankful to Dra. Aurora Pérez-Gramatges (LASURF–DQ–PUC- Rio) for the contact angle experiments. Analytical facilities from the Chemistry Department at PUC-Rio (CALPH PUC-Rio) were used.

ce:section.ce:para:
